items.correct: It proposed that an artist's choice and context could make an ordinary object art.
items.misconception: It displayed exceptional craftsmanship in porcelain.
items.random_wrong: It was the first sculpture ever shown in Paris.
items.why: Students look for craft skill, but the readymade's point was the idea and the act of designation, not making.

items.q: How does installation art differ from traditional sculpture?
items.correct: It transforms a whole space that the viewer enters and experiences, often site-specific and temporary.
items.misconception: It is just a large sculpture displayed on a plinth.
items.random_wrong: It must always include video screens.
items.why: Students scale sculpture up in their minds, missing that installation treats space and the viewer's experience as the medium.

items.q: In assemblage, why use found objects rather than newly made ones?
items.correct: Found objects carry histories and associations that become part of the work's meaning.
items.misconception: Found objects are used only to save money.
items.random_wrong: Galleries require artists to use recycled materials.
items.why: Students see found materials as a budget shortcut, missing the loaded associations that objects bring with them.
It's 1917. “1907” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Found object. “Lost object” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If an assemblage uses 6 unique found objects and an artist makes 8 such assemblages, how many objects in total?
items.why: The real figure is 48. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
